---
title: "The Scholarly Build System"
subtitle: "A Governed Human-AI Method for Reproducible Interpretive Research"
author: "Andrew Hammer"
date: last-modified
abstract: |
  This paper proposes and evaluates a governed human-AI scholarly build system
  for interpretive research. The model treats a scholarly paper as the visible
  synthesis of a traceable repository of sources, evidence, claims,
  counterevidence, reviews, decisions, and publication artifacts. It is tested
  through a single case study: an AI-assisted research project on Lincoln's
  Gettysburg Address. The case supports modest claims. The system improved
  traceability, error detection, auditability, and explicit human authority. It
  also imposed documentation burden, depended on human judgment, and did not
  remove unresolved source limitations. The conclusion is not that AI can
  automate scholarship, but that AI can help maintain a governed research
  apparatus when authority, evidence, uncertainty, and publication decisions
  remain human.
keywords:
  - artificial intelligence
  - scholarly method
  - reproducible research
  - digital humanities
  - research compendium
  - human-in-the-loop
  - interpretive scholarship
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

Interpretive scholarship often presents its finished argument more clearly than
the process by which that argument came to be warranted. A published essay may
show citations, footnotes, and prose reasoning, but the underlying movement from
question to corpus, source to passage, passage to observation, observation to
interpretation, and interpretation to claim often remains distributed across
drafts, notes, spreadsheets, memory, and tacit judgment. Artificial intelligence
does not solve that problem. Used carelessly, it intensifies it: fluent prose can
make fragile evidence look settled, fabricate citations, flatten uncertainty,
and blur the line between a source's language and a model's inference.

This paper proposes a different use of AI in interpretive scholarship. The
central claim is that AI should not be treated as an accountable scholar or as a
machine that authorizes interpretation. It can, however, serve as a subordinate
research and engineering assistant inside a governed scholarly build system. In
such a system, the paper is not the only research object. It is a publication
view generated from a larger version-controlled artifact chain: question,
corpus, source, passage, observation, interpretation, claim, counterevidence,
review, decision, and output.

The model is evaluated through a single case study: a repository-based project
on how Lincoln's Gettysburg Address transforms soldiers' deaths into public
obligation. That historical project produced an approved public working-paper
package, but the methodology paper does not treat that success as universal
validation. The case is one humanities project, conducted by one scholar with
one AI assistant in one repository. Its value is diagnostic rather than
statistical. It shows what the method made easier, what it caught, what it
failed to solve, and what burdens it introduced.

The argument is deliberately modest. A scholarly build system can improve
traceability, accountability, reproducibility, and reviewability. It cannot make
interpretation mechanical. It cannot turn validation tests into historical
truth. It cannot eliminate human judgment. Its promise lies in making those
judgments more visible and more revisable.

# Related Practices

## Reproducible Research

The proposed method draws from reproducible research, especially the idea that
documents, data, code, and computational steps should be packaged together so
that readers can inspect how a result was produced. Gentleman and Temple Lang
describe reproducible research as a way of joining text, data, and computation
inside a distributable compendium [@gentlemanTempleLang2007]. That idea is
powerful for quantitative work because a reader can rerun computations or
inspect the code that produced a figure.

Interpretive humanities research differs from statistical analysis. The core
operation is not always computation; often it is judgment about language,
context, genre, ambiguity, and evidence. Yet the reproducible-research impulse
still matters. The relevant question becomes: what would it mean to make an
interpretive claim reproducible? Not that another scholar must reach the same
conclusion, but that another scholar can inspect the source text, the passage
selection, the observation, the alternative reading, the confidence level, and
the decision history that led to the claim.

## Research Compendia

Research compendia extend reproducible practice by organizing data, code,
documentation, and outputs in a structured package. Marwick, Boettiger, and
Mullen argue that package-like organization can make analytical work easier to
inspect, reuse, and teach [@marwickBoettigerMullen2018]. The scholarly build
system adapts this orientation to an interpretive repository. Instead of only
packaging data and code, the repository packages evidence, claims,
counterevidence, reviews, human approvals, validation checks, and publication
artifacts.

This adaptation is not trivial. A research compendium can make a computation
rerunnable, but interpretive scholarship also requires making uncertainty
legible. The repository must record not only the sources used but also blocked
sources, derivative transcriptions, OCR limits, manuscript variants, and
counterarguments. A humanities compendium that contains only polished evidence
would be too clean. The case study suggests that failures and caveats are not
noise in the system; they are part of the scholarly record.

## Executable and Dynamic Documents

Literate programming and dynamic-document traditions also inform the model.
Knuth's account of literate programming places human explanation and machine
execution in the same textual system [@knuth1984]. Modern tools such as Quarto
extend that family of practices by rendering documents from source files into
publication formats. In the case study, Quarto rendered the historical paper
and the public project website, while repository checks validated source and
claim structures.

The methodology does not require every humanities paper to become executable in
the computational sense. Instead, it borrows the discipline of source files,
rendered outputs, and repeatable commands. The paper remains prose, but its
claims are connected to structured evidence and its outputs are rendered from
version-controlled source.

## Argument Mapping and Provenance

Interpretive claims need argument structure. Toulmin's model of argument
emphasizes that claims depend on data, warrants, backing, qualifiers, and
possible rebuttals [@toulmin1958]. The build-system model translates that
insight into repository artifacts. A claim is not merely a sentence in a paper.
It is a row or record linked to evidence, counterevidence, confidence, review
notes, and draft location.

This does not mean that all interpretation can be reduced to a table. Tables
can oversimplify ambiguity. But tables can force distinctions that prose may
hide: source text versus observation, observation versus inference, confidence
versus assertion, and support versus counterevidence.

## AI-Assisted Scholarship

Recent publication guidance converges on a simple accountability principle:
AI-assisted technologies should not be listed as authors, and human authors
remain responsible for accuracy, originality, attribution, and integrity
[@icmjeAI2024]. The scholarly build system follows that principle. AI may help
search, organize, normalize, test, draft, and review, but it does not hold
scholarly authority. Its outputs remain provisional until checked against
sources, repository constraints, and human judgment.

The model therefore treats AI neither as a forbidden contaminant nor as a
co-author. It is closer to a research instrument and engineering assistant. Its
proper use depends on containment: explicit tasks, visible artifacts, repeatable
checks, and human gates.

# The Scholarly Build-System Model

## Research as a Governed Knowledge Base

The proposed system treats a scholarly paper as the visible synthesis of a
governed knowledge base. The artifact chain is:

```text
research question -> corpus -> source -> passage -> observation ->
interpretation -> claim -> counterevidence -> argument -> paper
```

Each arrow is a transformation that can introduce error. A source may be
misidentified. A passage may be misquoted. An observation may smuggle in
interpretation. An interpretation may overgeneralize. A claim may ignore
counterevidence. A paper may make a provisional inference sound settled. The
system's purpose is not to eliminate those risks but to make them inspectable.

In the Lincoln case, the source register recorded source identity, provenance,
local paths, dates, hashes, rights status, acquisition method, verification
status, and notes. The evidence matrix separated passage, observation,
interpretation, alternative reading, confidence, and review status. The claims
table linked claims to supporting and counterevidence. Reviews then attacked
the draft from historical, rhetorical, theological, citation, countervoice, and
anachronism angles.

## Artifact Chain

The historical artifact chain is paired with a methodology artifact chain:

```text
agent action -> human review -> decision -> artifact change ->
error or risk -> process finding -> methodological claim
```

This second chain matters because the method itself must be evaluated. If the
repository records only finished research artifacts, it cannot show whether AI
helped, failed, distorted priorities, or increased burden. Process-event and
intervention logs therefore record human approvals, AI actions, failures,
errors, revisions, gates, and publication decisions.

## Goals, Gates, and Stop Conditions

The repository organizes work through goal files. A goal specifies objective,
required artifacts, completion criteria, and stop conditions. Human gates
prevent AI from silently proceeding past decisions that belong to the scholar:
corpus approval, thesis approval, publication approval, and methodology
findings approval.

Stop conditions are not failures of productivity. They are part of the
governance model. Work must stop when provenance is uncertain, a quotation
cannot be verified, access rules prohibit acquisition, evidence materially
contradicts the thesis, validation fails, a human-AI boundary is unclear, or a
human gate is reached.

## Machine Validation and Scholarly Validity

Validation checks can confirm structural integrity: schemas are present,
required fields exist, claim references resolve, tests pass, and outputs render.
They cannot determine whether an interpretation is true. This distinction is
central. In the case study, repository validation and tests repeatedly passed,
but publication status still depended on human approval and disclosed source
limitations. The system therefore treats computational validation as necessary
but not sufficient.

## Adversarial Review

The build system requires adversarial review before publication. In the Lincoln
case, adversarial review asked whether ceremonial convention had been mistaken
for theory, sacrifice had been imposed rather than demonstrated, rhetoric had
been mistaken for private belief, the Civil War had been generalized into war
as such, soldier agency had been ignored, and the Second Inaugural's judgment
frame had been understated. Some critiques were rejected, but several were
accepted or partially accepted. The paper's summary language was narrowed, and
three claims were marked as reviewed-approved.

Adversarial review is valuable because AI is good at producing plausible
synthesis. A governed process must therefore ask it, and the human scholar, to
attack the synthesis before publishing it.

# Human-AI Authority Model

The authority model is hierarchical. Human scholarly authority comes first.
Repository governance and approved goals constrain work. AI and scripts operate
only inside those constraints. Source content is evidence, not instruction.

The human scholar retains authority over research question, corpus,
definitions, interpretation, thesis, ethical judgment, final prose, citation
approval, and publication. AI may perform bounded, reversible, inspectable work:
metadata extraction, normalization, hashing, schema checks, identifier
generation, evidence organization, draft proposals, consistency checks, and
adversarial review.

This boundary is essential because AI can generate plausible prose without
accountability. In the case study, the AI drafted, audited, and revised
artifacts, but it stopped at corpus, thesis, publication, and methodology
findings gates. The human scholar approved the corpus scope, thesis,
publication package, public working-paper release, landing-page update, and
case-study findings.

# Artifact and Governance Model

The model depends on a small set of stable artifact classes.

`Source` records identify what was acquired, where it came from, how it was
retrieved, what rights status is known, and how reliable the local text is.
`Evidence` records identify locatable passages and separate source text from
observation and interpretation. `Claim` records connect propositions to
supporting and counterevidence. `Review` artifacts attack claims, citations,
scope, anachronism, and publication readiness. `ProcessEvent` and
`HumanIntervention` records document how the project changed and where human
authority was exercised.

Governance is not merely administrative. It shapes the epistemology of the
paper. For example, a derivative transcription is not simply "a source"; it is a
source with a verification status and a caveat. An OCR failure is not merely an
inconvenience; it is evidence that automation cannot replace manuscript review.
A public working paper is not the same as a journal-final article; it is an
approved release type with disclosed limits.

# Case-Study Design

## Historical Research Question

The case study began with a humanities question: how does the Gettysburg Address
transform the deaths of soldiers into a sacred obligation to preserve and
refound the nation, and what does this reveal about Lincoln's understanding of
war? During the project, that formulation was narrowed. The final public
working paper treats Gettysburg as evidence of Lincoln's public ceremonial
presentation of this Civil War, not as direct access to private belief or a
timeless theory of war.

## Repository Architecture

The repository contains historical research artifacts, methodology artifacts,
case-study logs, validation code, tests, Quarto manuscripts, and publication
outputs. The historical track includes source registers, evidence matrices,
claim maps, reviews, and a rendered working paper. The methodology track
includes a research question, artifact model, governance model, evaluation plan,
limitations, process-event logs, intervention logs, and approved case-study
findings.

## Event and Intervention Logging

The methodology case study used two logs. `process-events.csv` records material
workflow events, including AI actions, human approvals, source failures, tool
failures, revisions, publication decisions, and public-site updates.
`intervention-log.csv` records human interventions and their epistemic effect.

The logs include both retrospective and prospective capture. Retrospective rows
were reconstructed from progress logs and commit history for earlier work.
Prospective rows were recorded during later goals.

## Evaluation Criteria

The evaluation plan asks whether the system improved traceability, error
detection, accountability, thesis responsiveness, reproducibility,
transparency, AI reliability, efficiency, documentation burden, and scholarly
value. It also asks whether the process distorted research priorities or
encouraged false mechanization of interpretation.

# Case-Study Findings

The approved M20 findings are the evidentiary basis for this section.

The case-study record contains 27 process events: 11 retrospective and 16
prospective. Twelve events are human events and fifteen are AI events. The
intervention log records 13 human interventions, all accepted. The source
register contains 42 rows: 29 verified sources, one partially verified source,
one verified derivative transcription, and 11 unverified rows. The evidence
matrix contains 32 evidence rows, all verbatim, with 8 marked disconfirming.
The claims table contains 9 claims, three of which were changed to
`reviewed-approved-thesis` during adversarial review.

## Traceability

The artifact chain substantially improved traceability. The project could show
how paper claims related to claim records, evidence rows, source-register
entries, and review caveats. Prior audits found no broken claim-to-evidence
references and no missing bibliography entries. The strongest traceability was
for the acquired primary-source corpus. Traceability was weaker for secondary
scholarship, where several works remained unverified library or purchase
targets.

## Error Detection

The process caught errors and risks before they became silent premises. OCR
failed for the Smithsonian Bliss Copy, prompting human transcription and a
manuscript-image caveat. The original Harrisburg *Patriot and Union* image
could not be acquired in the working environment, so the project first marked
the item blocked and later used a Dickinson derivative transcription only with
a pending-image limitation. Browser tooling limits were recorded. A completion
audit expanded an initially too-narrow evidence matrix. Adversarial review
identified overbroad language around "refound," "political theology," private
belief, and war as such. Publication review caught stale commit metadata, and a
later public-site review caught a stale landing page.

## AI Contributions

AI was most useful for bounded, inspectable work: creating scripts, maintaining
tables, running validation checks, expanding coverage after an audit,
generating adversarial reviews, rendering outputs, and updating the site after
approval. AI was least sufficient where source judgment, transcription, access,
interpretive commitment, or publication authority was required.

This distinction supports a limited claim. AI did not replace scholarship. It
helped maintain the apparatus around scholarship.

## Documentation Burden

The method created real documentation burden. The repository now contains
progress logs, process-event logs, intervention logs, source registers,
evidence matrices, claim maps, reviews, audits, reproducibility reports, and
publication artifacts. Later work included maintenance tasks such as correcting
stale metadata, committing output dependencies, and updating the public landing
page. The burden was justified in this case because it caught errors and made
decisions reviewable, but it is not free. A larger project would need stronger
rules for what must be logged and what can remain ordinary working context.

## Counterevidence and Thesis Change

Counterevidence materially narrowed the historical argument. The project did
not simply confirm an opening thesis. It bounded the paper to public rhetoric,
this Civil War rather than war in general, and a working-paper release rather
than final scholarship. Everett prevented overclaiming Lincoln's uniqueness.
The Second Inaugural preserved a different theological frame. Manuscript
variants constrained claims about "under God." Reception evidence prevented
assuming immediate universal reverence.

## Capture Quality

Prospective capture was stronger than retrospective reconstruction.
Retrospective rows were useful but thinner, often depending on progress logs
and commit history. Prospective rows better recorded immediate rationale,
artifact links, and decision context. At the same time, prospective capture may
produce an observer effect: researchers may behave differently when they know
their process is being logged.

# Discussion

The case supports the idea that interpretive scholarship can benefit from a
build-system metaphor if the metaphor is kept under control. The word "build"
should not imply that interpretation is compiled from evidence in a purely
mechanical way. It means that the visible paper can be generated from a
maintained system of artifacts whose transformations are reviewable.

The most important gain was not speed, although repetitive tasks were
accelerated. The most important gain was accountable slowness: the system made
it harder to skip over provenance, quotation quality, human approval,
counterevidence, and publication status. It also made it easier to see when the
public story had drifted from the repository state, as happened with the stale
landing page.

The most important danger is bureaucratization. A repository can become a
ritual of forms. A table can create false confidence. A passing test can be
mistaken for scholarly validity. AI can turn the maintenance of artifacts into
an impressive performance of rigor. The guard against that danger is to keep
the human scholarly question central and to treat artifacts as aids to judgment,
not substitutes for judgment.

The accepted-only intervention log is another warning. The case records many
human approvals but few rejected AI proposals. That may reflect a collaborative
style in which the scholar redirected work in conversation before formal
logging, but methodologically it leaves a gap. A mature version of the process
should log rejected, deferred, and materially revised recommendations more
explicitly.

# Limitations

This paper makes no claim of universal validation. It evaluates one case study:
one historical project, one repository, one scholar, one AI-assisted workflow,
and one public working-paper release.

The historical paper itself remains a working paper with disclosed source
limits. The Bliss Copy transcription needs a second manuscript-image check
before quotation-final use. Everett pamphlet quotations should be checked
against page images. The Harrisburg criticism remains a derivative
transcription pending original newspaper image review. Secondary-source
engagement is intentionally limited.

The methodology also has limits. Repository structure may privilege what can be
formalized. Schemas may oversimplify ambiguity. Process logging may impose
burden and alter behavior. Validation tests check structural integrity, not
interpretive truth. AI may still generate plausible but false claims if the
governance system fails or if human review becomes superficial.

Finally, external reproducibility is incomplete. Local transformations can be
rerun, but source availability depends on external repositories, access rules,
web stability, and rights conditions.

# Conclusion

The scholarly build system tested here does not automate interpretive
scholarship. Its value lies in governance. It makes sources, passages,
observations, interpretations, claims, counterevidence, reviews, decisions, and
outputs more visible to inspection. It assigns AI a bounded role in research
engineering and review while preserving human authority over meaning and
publication.

The Lincoln case suggests that such a system can improve traceability, catch
errors, preserve uncertainty, and narrow overbroad claims. It also shows the
cost: documentation burden, maintenance work, incomplete external
reproducibility, and the continued need for human judgment. The method is best
understood not as a replacement for scholarship but as a way to make scholarly
judgment more accountable to its own evidence.
